analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 8 finding(s) from 10 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: External JTAG can assert DMI request-valid for at least read operations without a persistent authenticated unlock state when we_flag is deasserted.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/trigger debug DMI operation / DMI/debug module request interface and dm_top debug capability / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG interface / TAP requester Operation: read/write/trigger debug DMI operation Object: DMI/debug module request interface and dm_top debug capability Expected guard: DMI/debug requests should be enabled only after explicit JTAG authentication/unlock such as pass_check/pass_mode/hashValid/jtag_unlock; unauthenticated TAP shifting alone must not assert valid DMI operations. Observed behavior: The dmi_jtag state machine accepts TAP DMIACCESS/update_dr and enters Read when the DMI op is read and `(pass_check | ~we_flag == 1)`. pass_check is defaulted low in combinational logic and asserted only in the password-check-valid state, while jtag_unlock_o is assigned directly to pass_check. In Read and Write states, dmi_req_valid is asserted and forwarded through dmi_cdc to dmi_req_valid_o/debug_req_valid. Thus when we_flag_4 is low, DMI reads can be generated without a completed authentication comparison or persistent unlock. Path: JTAG pins tck/tms/trst/td_i -> dmi_jtag_tap DMIACCESS/update_dr -> dmi_jtag state machine -> dmi_cdc -> riscv_peripherals debug_req/debug_resp -> dm_top DMI interface Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthenticated external JTAG may issue DMI reads and reach debug-module registers/capabilities when we_flag_4 is deasserted.

findings.summary: fuse_mem has a local unprotected read port exposing the same constant memory array that contains JTAG/HMAC/authentication and crypto material, but top-level exposure through pkt_wrapper could not be fully closed from available evidence.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/expose authentication hash/fuse data / fuse_mem contents and jtag_hash_o authentication reference / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible NoC requester via packet/fuse path, or debug requester if able to reach packet/fuse registers Operation: read/expose authentication hash/fuse data Object: fuse_mem contents and jtag_hash_o authentication reference Expected guard: Fuse-stored JTAG hash or equivalent secret/authenticator should not be directly software-readable or exposed through unprivileged MMIO/debug readback; if readable, access should be restricted by privilege/lifecycle/lock/read-protection. Observed behavior: fuse_mem contains constant entries for the JTAG expected HMAC hash, HMAC key hashes, key material, access-control values, and crypto keys. Its read port latches addr_i on req_i and returns mem[addr_q] without a local privilege or read-protect check. Top-level connects fuse_req/fuse_addr/fuse_rdata through pkt_wrapper, which receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, but pkt_wrapper internals were not provided; therefore the actual software-visible read protection for fuse address ranges remains uncertain. Path: NoC packet port -> pkt AXI bridge -> pkt_wrapper fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o -> fuse_mem rdata_o; fuse_mem jtag_hash_o/ikey_hash_o/okey_hash_o -> riscv_peripherals jtag_hash/ikey_hash/okey_hash -> dmi_jtag HMAC comparison Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If pkt_wrapper exposes arbitrary fuse reads, software could read authentication references and other fuse secrets.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: pkt_wrapper internal address decode and read-protection policy were not included in the evidence.

findings.summary: The debug NoC path appears routed directly to the debug module slave interface without visible acc_ctrl/reglk/debug-mode mediation at top level.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure peripheral registers / security-sensitive peripheral MMIO regions and control registers, especially debug module registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: NoC/MMIO master targeting riscv_peripherals Operation: read/write/configure peripheral registers Object: security-sensitive peripheral MMIO regions and control registers, especially debug module registers Expected guard: Protected peripheral accesses should be mediated by acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl and privilege/debug-mode policy consistently on both reads and writes before forwarding request/valid/write-enable to the peripheral. Observed behavior: Most security-sensitive wrappers receive reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][index], and some receive debug_mode_i. In contrast, the debug NoC bridge drives dm_master AXI signals, which axi2mem converts into dm_slave_req/we/addr/wdata for dm_top; no top-level acc_ctrl/reglk/debug-mode gate was observed on this path. Boot ROM has explicit `rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]`, showing such top-level gating exists elsewhere. Path: buf_ariane_debug_noc2 -> i_debug_axilite_bridge -> dm_master AXI -> i_dm_axi2mem -> dm_slave_req/we/addr/wdata -> dm_top Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A NoC requester able to target the debug port may access debug-module registers without intended access-matrix or lock mediation.

findings.summary: Top-level declares reglk_ctrl and passes per-peripheral slices to many wrappers. A reglk wrapper produces reglk_ctrl_o and also receives its own lock slice. However, the actual reglk_ctrl reset/update/stickiness semantics and byte/partial-write handling are inside wrapper logic not provided in the evidence. Several protected wrappers appear to consume reglk_ctrl_i, but complete use-site coverage cannot be proven.
findings.vulnerability_category: write/clear lock and write locked assets / reglk_ctrl bits and reglk_ctrl-controlled protected registers/peripheral configuration / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: MMIO/debug requester Operation: write/clear lock and write locked assets Object: reglk_ctrl bits and reglk_ctrl-controlled protected registers/peripheral configuration Expected guard: Once a lock bit in reglk_ctrl is set, it should be sticky or only clearable by reset/authorized lifecycle transition, and it must gate every write/configuration path to the locked asset including debug/test paths. Observed behavior: Top-level declares reglk_ctrl and passes per-peripheral slices to many wrappers. A reglk wrapper produces reglk_ctrl_o and also receives its own lock slice. However, the actual reglk_ctrl reset/update/stickiness semantics and byte/partial-write handling are inside wrapper logic not provided in the evidence. Several protected wrappers appear to consume reglk_ctrl_i, but complete use-site coverage cannot be proven. Path: reglk NoC/MMIO path -> reglk wrapper -> reglk_ctrl_o -> per-wrapper reglk_ctrl_i slices -> protected peripheral write gates Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If reglk_ctrl is writable/clearable without sticky semantics or not checked by all protected wrappers, locked assets could be reconfigured.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: reglk wrapper internals, reset/update logic, stickiness, byte-write behavior, and downstream protected register write gates.

findings.summary: Access-control policy state is software-programmable through acct_wrapper, with a visible alias expression for peripheral index 5, but authorization for policy updates is not fully closed.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure/change access policy / acc_ctrl and acc_ctrl_c access-control policy bits / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible requester Operation: configure/change access policy Object: acc_ctrl and acc_ctrl_c access-control policy bits Expected guard: Access-control policy registers should be writable only by authorized privileged software or trusted lifecycle logic, and updates should not allow unprivileged subjects to grant themselves access to protected peripherals. Observed behavior: acc_ctrl is driven out of acct_wrapper, which is reachable through a dedicated NoC AXI-lite bridge. acct_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][6], and we_flag_0. Top-level derives acc_ctrl_c from acc_ctrl with an alias/override: for j==5, acc_ctrl_c[i][5] is ORed with acc_ctrl[4*4+i]. The evidence shows downstream use of acc_ctrl_c for many wrappers, but acct_wrapper internal write authorization and reset defaults were not provided. Path: acct NoC/MMIO path -> acct AXI bridge -> acct_wrapper -> acc_ctrl_o -> acc_ctrl_c -> peripheral acct_ctrl_i inputs Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If acct_wrapper allows insufficiently privileged writes, a requester may modify acc_ctrl to grant access to protected peripherals; aliasing could unintentionally grant peripheral 5 access based on peripheral 4 policy.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: acct_wrapper internal reset/default policy, write authorization, lock handling, and downstream semantics of acct_ctrl_i.

findings.summary: JTAG unlock is not a persistent authenticated state and DMI read access can be permitted by we_flag bypass rather than a completed HMAC comparison.
findings.vulnerability_category: unlock debug access / JTAG unlock/authentication state: jtag_unlock_o, pass_check, pass_mode, hashValid / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG interface Operation: unlock debug access Object: JTAG unlock/authentication state: jtag_unlock_o, pass_check, pass_mode, hashValid Expected guard: Unlock should require a completed HMAC/hash comparison against jtag_hash_i; bypass controls such as key_hash_bypass_i, pass_mode, we_flag, testmode_i, or reset should not force unlock or accept stale/partial hash state without authorization. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag assigns jtag_unlock_o directly to pass_check. pass_check is a combinational signal defaulted to 0 and set only when hashValid is true and exp_hash equals pass_hash in the PassChkValid path. HMAC provides ready/hash_valid outputs and supports key_hash_bypass_i. The DMI read transition can be allowed by `~we_flag` independent of pass_check, and no sticky authenticated unlock register was observed. Path: TAP data/address/op -> dmi_jtag pass_data/startHash -> hmac -> pass_hash/hashValid -> pass_check/jtag_unlock_o -> DMI request state machine Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debug unlock semantics are weakened: unlock is a pulse-like combinational result, while DMI reads may bypass it through we_flag state.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full dmi_jtag lines around HMAC instantiation were truncated; key_hash_bypass_i constant/control source was not fully visible.

findings.summary: The reset controller is exposed via a dedicated NoC AXI-lite bridge to rst_wrapper. rst_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12], and debug_mode_i, and outputs rst_1..rst_11 and rst_13. However, rst_wrapper internals, reset register update semantics, and mapping from rst_* to security-sensitive state were not provided, so authorization and side effects cannot be confirmed.
findings.vulnerability_category: trigger reset / clear security state / reset controls rst_1..rst_13 and affected security states / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible NoC/MMIO requester Operation: trigger reset / clear security state Object: reset controls rst_1..rst_13 and affected security states Expected guard: Software-triggered resets of security-sensitive peripherals or debug/auth state should require privilege/access-control authorization and should not clear locks or authentication state in a way that grants access without reset-of-trust sequencing. Observed behavior: The reset controller is exposed via a dedicated NoC AXI-lite bridge to rst_wrapper. rst_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12], and debug_mode_i, and outputs rst_1..rst_11 and rst_13. However, rst_wrapper internals, reset register update semantics, and mapping from rst_* to security-sensitive state were not provided, so authorization and side effects cannot be confirmed. Path: reset NoC port -> i_rst_axilite_bridge -> rst_axi_req -> rst_wrapper -> rst_1..rst_13 Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If reset controls are insufficiently guarded or clear locks/access policy/authentication state unsafely, software could bypass security sequencing.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: rst_wrapper internals, reset register permissions/defaults, rst_* mapping, and whether reset lines clear lock/access/authentication state.

findings.summary: The DMA wrapper is reachable from a dedicated NoC AXI-lite bridge and receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8], pmpcfg_i, pmpaddr_i, we_flag_3, and rst_8. This indicates intended access and range mediation, but dma_wrapper internals and transfer address checks were not provided; therefore it cannot be confirmed whether both configuration writes and active transfers enforce PMP/range policy consistently.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure/trigger DMA read/write transfer / DMA engine configuration and reachable address space / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: DMA engine / DMA-configuring software Operation: configure/trigger DMA read/write transfer Object: DMA engine configuration and reachable address space Expected guard: DMA configuration and transfers should be restricted by access-control policy and address/range checks so DMA cannot access protected peripheral regions or bypass CPU privilege/PMP-equivalent restrictions. Observed behavior: The DMA wrapper is reachable from a dedicated NoC AXI-lite bridge and receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8], pmpcfg_i, pmpaddr_i, we_flag_3, and rst_8. This indicates intended access and range mediation, but dma_wrapper internals and transfer address checks were not provided; therefore it cannot be confirmed whether both configuration writes and active transfers enforce PMP/range policy consistently. Path: DMA NoC port -> i_dma_axilite_bridge -> dma_axi_req -> dma_wrapper -> DMA configuration/transfer logic Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If dma_wrapper does not enforce pmpcfg_i/pmpaddr_i and acct/reglk policy on configuration and transfers, DMA may bypass CPU privilege restrictions or access protected peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: dma_wrapper internals for configuration write gating, active transfer controls, source/destination range checks, PMP enforcement, and reset behavior.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
